open_questions: Is wife Mary Fenn a daughter of Benjamin Fenn (X00010)? X00010 records list "Mary (bapt 1647)" among children of first marriage to Sarah Baldwin. If confirmed, this creates a Fenn cross-connection through the Leete line.

sources.notes: Mary identified as "only daughter of William and Mary (Fenn) Leete"

open_questions: Is wife Mary Fenn a daughter of Benjamin Fenn (X00010)? X00010 records list "Mary (bapt 1647)" among children of first marriage to Sarah Baldwin. If confirmed, this creates a Fenn cross-connection through the Leete line.

sources.notes: Mary identified as "only daughter of William and Mary (Fenn) Leete"

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter married Judge James Hooker of Guilford
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Fenn was a daughter of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford. Her daughter Mary Leete married James Hooker, a grandson of Thomas Hooker and the first probate judge of the Guilford district.

narrative_blocks.header: She remarried a King William’s War captain
narrative_blocks.body: After William Leete died in 1687, Mary married Captain Stephen Bradley of Guilford, who served as an officer of dragoons and a trainband captain during King William’s War.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father was William Leete Jr., son of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and Colony of Connecticut. Her first cousin Mercy Leete (daughter of her uncle Andrew) also married a Hooker man. Two of the Governor's granddaughters, through different sons, both married into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a Connecticut governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was a granddaughter of Gov. William Leete, who governed both the New Haven and Connecticut colonies. She married Judge James Hooker in 1691 and they settled at Guilford.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford; actual grave uncertain. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

cross_connections.display_label: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies
was his father-in-law; son of Governor William Leete

narrative_blocks.header: He married the granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was the daughter of William Leete Jr. and the granddaughter of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut. James Hooker, born a Farmington pastor's son, married into one of the most distinguished families in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: First Probate Judge — presided over the Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first Probate Judge of the Guilford district and repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly, serving for a number of sessions as its presiding officer. EH notes he "became a very prominent man at Guilford."

narrative_blocks.header: His only son died at 21, name extinct
narrative_blocks.body: EH notes he "had but one son who died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name." That son was William, Yale class of 1722, who died at 21.

career.notes: First judge of the Guilford probate district; also a town magistrate.
Repeatedly represented Guilford; presiding officer for several sessions.

bio.title: Honorable
bio.bio_blurb: First probate judge of the Guilford district

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband succeeded Daniel as Yale's second tutor

narrative_blocks.header: Married Yale's first B.A. graduate
narrative_blocks.body: On December 6, 1720 she married Rev. John Hart of East Guilford as his third wife. Hart had been Yale's first B.A. graduate (1703) and second tutor — succeeding her uncle Daniel Hooker, who had been Yale's first tutor.

narrative_blocks.header: Three of her six children survived to marry
narrative_blocks.body: With John Hart she had six children — four sons and two daughters. Two sons died young and infant Mary lived only three months. Sons Thomas (1723-1813) and Benjamin (1725-1804) carried the line, while daughter Sarah married three times — twice to Yale graduates.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Hooker married Thomas Smith of the New York Highlands. Her younger sister Mehitable married his brother, Rev. Dr. John Smith, joining the two families twice over.

enslaved_persons.name: Peleg
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Bequeathed in Sarah's will to daughter Anna Ruggles, then to grandson Nathaniel Ruggles Jr. if unsold at Anna's death. Probate inventory lists "Negro boy Peleg, 65 pounds."
enslaved_persons.source: Sarah Hooker Bartlett probate, Guilford, Connecticut

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Guilford CT; grave marker effaced. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Her will bequeathed an enslaved man named Peleg
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker Bartlett's will, proved after her death in 1769, left to her daughter an enslaved man named Peleg, listed among the property of her Guilford estate.

burial.plot_notes: Alderbrook Cemetery, Guilford, New Haven County, Connecticut

education.notes: Yale graduate — class year not confirmed

narrative_blocks.header: Yale graduate, dead at twenty-one
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale around 1722 and died March 20, 1724 in Guilford at age 21, unmarried. Edward Hooker noted he 'died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name.'

narrative_blocks.header: The last Hooker of the Guilford line
narrative_blocks.body: He was the only son of Hon. James Hooker, the Probate Judge of Guilford. With his early death, the Hooker surname disappeared from Guilford within a single generation of arriving.

sources.short: Smith-Hooker Genealogy by Della Bishop, 1936, Ithaca NY

cross_connections.display_label: Mehitable Hooker Smith is the great-grandmother of Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with Rev. John Smith of Rye
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. John Smith of Rye, New York, and had twelve children between 1727 and 1750. The family lived in Westchester County during the years just before the Revolution turned that border country into a no-man's land.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandmother of Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandson Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran the Culper Ring for George Washington — the network that exposed Benedict Arnold's 1780 treason. Tallmadge was a classmate of Nathan Hale and a fellow Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Abigail at Washington's White Plains headquarters
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Abigail Smith married Jacob Purdy, whose Purchase, NY house served as Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains in 1776 and again in 1778. The Purdy descendants would carry the line through several Patriot–Loyalist conflicts.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Hooker married Rev. Dr. John Smith of Rye. Her elder sister Hannah had married his brother Thomas, so two Hooker sisters wed two Smith brothers.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
Interred at the Ancient Burying Ground — same ground as Thomas Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: sheltered the regicides at Milford, as Leete did
His granddaughter married the first Guilford Probate Judge.

notable.notable_blurb: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies

narrative_blocks.header: The only man to govern both New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: He served Guilford as town clerk from 1639, rose to Governor of New Haven Colony (1661-1664), and then Governor of the Colony of Connecticut (1676-1683) — the only person ever to hold both offices. He is buried at the Ancient Burying Ground in Hartford — the same ground as Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He used the Sabbath and slow horses
narrative_blocks.body: When royal agents arrived demanding the regicides Goffe and Whalley, he read the King's warrant aloud in public — alerting everyone to the agents' purpose — then furnished horses so slowly that the Sabbath intervened before they could depart. By Monday the men were hidden. Roger Newton did the same in Milford; Roger Hooker did the same in Hartford — three men in the same civic network, the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughters married into the Hooker family — two branches
narrative_blocks.body: His son William Leete Jr. fathered Mary Leete, who married Hon. James Hooker of Guilford. His son Andrew Leete fathered Mercy Leete, who married Samuel Hooker of Kensington. Two of his granddaughters independently married into the Hooker family in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Guilford's founding settlers
narrative_blocks.body: Leete left England in 1639 to escape the persecution of Puritans he had witnessed as a court clerk. He was among the twenty-five who signed the plantation covenant at sea and helped found Guilford, becoming one of the seven pillars of its church.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept Guilford's records for over twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: Leete served as town clerk of Guilford from 1639 until 1662, alongside terms as magistrate and deputy. The long climb through colony offices carried him eventually to the governorship.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. John Payne of Southoe, Huntingdonshire.

bio.bio_blurb: First wife of Gov. William Leete; mother of his ten children

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of all ten Leete children
narrative_blocks.body: Anna Payne, daughter of Rev. John Payne of Southoe, married William Leete in England in 1636 and crossed to Guilford with him. She bore all ten of his children and died in 1668, before he rose to the Connecticut governorship.
